title: First time after pulling an image, running a rootless container with --userns auto is ~250x slower than subsequent times.
body:
### Issue Description

After pulling a new image, the first time it is run rootlessly with `--userns auto`, it takes 250x more time to start than on the 2nd (and subsequent) run(s). Running rootfully (with `--userns auto`) or rootlessly without `--userns auto` does not cause this.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
0. Don't be root
1. Pull a new image
2. Run a container with `--userns auto`
3. Die from old age

### Describe the results you received

It took over 3 minutes to start the container. When run as a systemd service (via a `.container` quadlet) this causes a systemd service timeout.

### Describe the results you expected

It starts as fast as without `--userns auto`.

### podman info output

```bash
$ podman version
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.9.3
API Version:  4.9.3
Go Version:   go1.22.2
Built:        Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

Running Ubuntu 24.04 on x86_64

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

```bash
$ podman system df -v
Images space usage:

REPOSITORY  TAG         IMAGE ID    CREATED     SIZE        SHARED SIZE  UNIQUE SIZE  CONTAINERS

Containers space usage:

CONTAINER ID  IMAGE       COMMAND     LOCAL VOLUMES  SIZE        CREATED     STATUS      NAMES

Local Volumes space usage:

VOLUME NAME  LINKS       SIZE
$ time podman pull \
  myregistry.internal/myimage:mytag \
  --creds=[username] \
&& time podman run \
  --name=mycontainer \
  --userns auto \
  --detach \
  myregistry.internal/myimage:mytag
Password: Trying to pull myregistry.internal/myimage:mytag...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying blob [hash] done   |
Copying config [hash] done   |
Writing manifest to image destination
[hash]

real	0m16.058s
user	0m14.379s
sys	0m5.324s
[hash]

real	3m12.216s
user	0m3.844s
sys	0m17.136s
$ podman stop mycontainer
mycontainer
$ podman container prune
WARNING! This will remove all non running containers.
Are you sure you want to continue? [y/N] y
[hash]
$ time podman run \
  --name=mycontainer \
  --userns auto \
  --detach \
  myregistry.internal/myimage:mytag
[hash]

real	0m0.758s
user	0m0.082s
sys	0m0.053s
```

comments.body:
Creating containers is slow when  these conditions are all met:

* rootless Podman is used
* a modified UID/GID mapping is used
* _native overlayfs_ is used
* no container has yet been created with the specified container image and UID/GID mapping


For details, see the Podman performance guide

* https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/7808625785b13d07b1e2005f7510b7b61a5d3dc7/docs/tutorials/performance.md?plain=1#L81

To work around the problem, you could use `fuse-overlayfs`.

I did a very basic speed comparison of `native overlay`  to `fuse-overalyfs` here:
https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1749#issuecomment-2417893449

Yes this is expected behavior, on first use the image must be basically copied in full to chown every single file according to the userns. As @eriksjolund points out use fuse-overlayfs if this is a problem for you but then you have a higher runtime overhead.

> Yes this is expected behavior, on first use the image must be basically copied in full to chown every single file according to the userns. As [@eriksjolund](https://github.com/eriksjolund) points out use fuse-overlayfs if this is a problem for you but then you have a higher runtime overhead.

I don't quite understand how this explains the difference in behaviour between rootless and rootful podman usage.

As root podman will use idmap mounts from the kernel which allow us to dynamically remap the ids. But the kernel restricts that to the root user only and AFAIK there are no plan to allow idmapped mounts as normal user.
